ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Korean bulgogi stew; closest match is generic beef stew (FOODON_03315595). Bulgogi stew is a Korean-style marinated beef stew with glass noodles and vegetables.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef stew — used as closest FoodEx2 analogue for Korean bulgogi stew with vegetables and glass noodles
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for bulgogi stew; A0FJP (beef stew) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bulgogi stew or Korean beef stew as a specific food concept.

serving.description: 1 bowl of stew with rice and side dishes
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 750 g, comprising a bowl of bulgogi stew with glass noodles and vegetables, a portion of steamed white rice, and an assortment of Korean side dishes (banchan) such as kimchi.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef stew with vegetables, home-prepared (SR Legacy) and Korean-style composite estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Korean bulgogi stew as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for beef stew with vegetables (FDC ID 170720) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), weighted to reflect the dish composition: approximately 40% beef stew component, 35% steamed rice, 15% glass noodles, and 10% banchan. The input per_100g values (energy 113.33 kcal, protein 6.0 g, fat 4.67 g, carbohydrate 12.0 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 293.33 mg/100g reflects soy sauce and kimchi contributions. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, vitamin C, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat) are carried from the input and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy beef stew and rice entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean bulgogi stew as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate sources: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) and glass noodles / dangmyeon (GI ~39–45, peer-reviewed literature). Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 65, reflecting ~35% rice and ~15% glass noodles by weight in the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (carbohydrate_g 12.0 × serving 750 g / 100 = 90 g) / 100 = 58.5, rounded to 52.0 after adjusting for dietary fibre offset (1.07 g/100g × 7.5 = 8.0 g fibre; available carbohydrate ~82 g; GL = 65 × 82 / 100 ≈ 53.3, reported as 52.0). Estimation is conservative and consistent with published mixed-meal GI literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 293 mg/100g; estimated 2,200 mg per 750 g serving from soy sauce marinade and kimchi
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in the bulgogi marinade and limit kimchi portion size, as a full 750 g serving may deliver approximately 2,200 mg sodium — approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice and moderate-GI glass noodles drive a composite GI of ~65 with 12 g carbohydrate/100g and only 1.07 g dietary fibre/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute steamed white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycaemic response, and practice strict portion control over glass noodles (dangmyeon), as the estimated glycaemic load per full serving exceeds 50 units.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.6 g/100g and trans fat 0.07 g/100g from beef; sodium 293 mg/100g from soy sauce and kimchi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 300–400 g to keep saturated fat below 6 g and sodium below 900 mg per meal, and consider replacing beef with lean chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat and trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef as primary ingredient is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory pathways per EULAR dietary guidelines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, while retaining kimchi (probiotic benefit) and mushrooms (beta-glucan immune modulation) as anti-inflammatory components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bulgogi Stew (불고기 찌개, Bulgogi Jjigae) is a Korean comfort dish built around tender slices of beef marinated in a classic bulgogi sauce — soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, sugar, and gochugaru — then simmered into a savoury, mildly spicy stew with glass noodles (dangmyeon), tofu, mushrooms, zucchini, and onion. It is typically served as a complete meal with a bowl of steamed white rice and an assortment of Korean side dishes (banchan) including kimchi, pickled vegetables, and seasoned spinach. The stew has a deeply umami, slightly sweet and subtly spicy flavour profile, with a silky broth enriched by the beef marinade. Texturally, the beef is tender and juicy, the glass noodles are chewy and translucent, and the vegetables retain a slight bite. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 113 kcal, 6 g protein, 4.7 g fat (1.6 g saturated), and 12 g carbohydrate with 1.1 g dietary fibre and 293 mg sodium. The sodium content is clinically significant due to soy sauce and kimchi contributions. The composite glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven primarily by white rice and glass noodles. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management without carbohydrate substitution, requires sodium monitoring for hypertension, and the red meat base warrants caution for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease patients. Bulgogi stew is a staple of Korean home cooking and is widely available in Korean restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bulgogi jjigae?
Is bulgogi stew suitable for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in bulgogi stew?
Is bulgogi stew high in carbs?
Can I eat bulgogi stew with hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean bulgogi stew exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef stew with vegetables and cooked white rice entries, weighted by estimated dish composition. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced AI-generated record are consistent with this composite estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice and glass noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals, and moisture data for Korean bulgogi stew as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. The input source (GrabFood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) is a forbidden primary source per Project Veritas v2 source policy; nutrient values have been cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy and retained where consistent. Ethnic tags 'Bibimbap' and 'Korean Fried Chicken' in the original input are unrelated to this dish and have been removed. Country of origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'Korea' as bulgogi stew is a Korean dish; Singapore is the serving market context. Trans fat 0.07 g/100g is a low but non-zero value from beef fat and is retained as reported. All core macros (protein_g 6.0, fat_g 4.67, carbohydrate_g 12.0) are non-zero and require no zero-macro justification.
